“Ōmura’s version is flawless,” she said. “The guild has already approved the bronze edition. It will stand in the palace garden within a moon. The court will never question it.”

“The court doesn’t carve,” Takeo said. “Men do. Men with hands that remember.”

Aya looked at the brush on the pedestal. The crane feathers trembled with the draft from the screened windows.

“He’s watching you,” she said.

Takeo didn’t look. “Ōmura always watches.”

“Not just him. The court. They’re waiting for you to kneel. To weep. To admit the error of your pride.”

“Pride doesn’t carve truth.”

“Truth doesn’t pay imperial patronage,” she said. “And patronage is what keeps men alive in this city.”

A guard coughed beyond the hanging silk. The court’s attention shifted like wind through reeds. Ōmura stood near the far dais, his profile sharp against the lantern glow. His fingers were still—no tremor now. He held a brush in his left hand, the imperial seal in the right.

Aya’s gaze flicked to the maquette, then to the brush.

“Sign it,” Takeo said.

She didn’t move. “Sign what?”

“Your name beside mine.”

Her lips parted. “You want me to...?”

“Claim it as yours,” he said. “Or leave it to rot with his.”

The court had begun to murmur. A Keiha master raised a fan in mock salute. Ōmura’s eyes narrowed, just for a heartbeat.

Aya picked up the brush. Its tip touched the parchment only for a second before the court’s silence swallowed the sound.

She signed.

Her name curled across the imperial plaque, beneath the carved lines of Takeo’s own signature from years ago. The brush didn’t shake. The ink didn’t bleed.

For a breath, nothing happened.

Then the court erupted.

A Keiha elder dropped his teacup. Porcelain shattered across marble. Ōmura’s face went slack, his left hand flinching as if struck. A clerk clutched his ledger, pages fluttering like wounded birds.

Aya didn’t look at Takeo. She kept her gaze on the plaque, on the two names, one old and one new, both claiming the same stone.

Takeo folded the maquette back into the linen and returned it to his sleeve. The court’s voices rose into a storm—some denouncing, some weeping, some laughing with the kind of relief reserved for those who have watched a trapped beast finally break free.

Ōmura stepped forward, breaking the chaos. His voice cut through the hall, clear and cutting.

“Lady Fujimoto!” he called. “You are a concubine of the Inner Palace. You do not carve! You do not sign!”

She turned slowly, her gray kosode catching the lantern light. “No. But I do witness.”

Takeo moved to the edge of the dais, his sandals silent against the polished wood. He watched Ōmura’s face redden, watched the imperial clerks scramble for the ledgers, watched the court realize the ground beneath them had just cracked open.

Ōmura’s hand shot out, pointing at the plaque. “This is forgery! Fraud! The court will—”

“Will do nothing,” Aya said. “Because the plaque now bears two names. And the court has always revered duality. Harmony. Balance.”

The hall fell again to silence.

Takeo met Aya’s eyes for the first time. She didn’t smile. She didn’t triumph. She only looked back at him, her expression unreadable beneath the shifting lantern light.

The court erupted once more.

Not in denial. Not in rage.

In applause.

Chapter 15

The imperial garden stretched beyond the screened windows, a tangle of maple and pine, the moss beneath their feet still damp from last night’s rain. Haruto Ōmura stood with his back to the lanterns, his shadow long across the stepping stones. He wore no formal robe today, only the indigo linen of a scholar, its hem frayed at the cuffs. His left hand rested on the repaired shoulder of the Bodhisattva Jizō, fingers splayed against the gold-leaf seam where the crack had been.

Takeo moved between the pines, his bare feet silent on the moss. The acolyte—fresh-faced, wide-eyed—trailed behind, hands clasped behind his back. The boy had been given strict orders not to speak unless addressed.

Ōmura didn’t turn as Takeo approached. “You’ve done remarkable work,” he said. “The guild praised the repair. Called it the finest example of kintsugi in a generation.”

“It was already whole,” Takeo replied. “Only the gold was missing.”

Ōmura’s thumb traced the seam. The gold caught the pale morning light, a thin, luminous scar. “You ever wonder why we hide the breaks?”

“Stones don’t have cracks. Only people do.”

A flicker crossed Ōmura’s face. Something like recognition. Something like regret. Then it was gone. “The court will unveil the new bronze Jizō next month. Commissioned from the East Mountain Quarry. They want it flawless. No seams. No shame.”

Takeo’s eyes stayed on the statue. “Flawless is a lie the living carve into stone.”

Ōmura exhaled through his nose. A short laugh, almost a snort. “You always had a poet’s tongue for a sword’s hand.”

“And you always preferred the sword to the poem.”

The acolyte shifted his weight. A twig snapped beneath his sandal. Ōmura’s gaze flicked to the boy, then back to the statue. “You brought him to witness?”

“He’s seen the repairs before,” Takeo said. “But not the cracks they hide.”

Ōmura’s fingers curled slightly, pressing harder into the gold. His knuckles whitened. “You think I don’t know what you’re doing? Parsing my words like I’m some village idiot?”

“I think you know exactly what you carved,” Takeo said. “And what you signed.”

A long silence stretched between them, thick as the incense smoke curling from the garden braziers. The acolyte held his breath.

Then Ōmura spoke, voice low. “You think this changes anything? A broken statue. A name on a plaque. The court cares about harmony, not honesty. They’ll erase both by moonrise.”

“Not if the stone speaks first.”

Ōmura’s head tilted, just slightly. “The stone is silent.”

“Then why are you trembling?”

For the first time, Takeo saw it—the tremor in Ōmura’s left hand, the one that had never fully healed after the duel with the Minamoto champion. It ran through his fingers, a faint vibration, like the hum of a plucked lute string. The gold beneath his fingertips remained unbroken. Uncracked. Whole.

Ōmura didn’t pull his hand away. If anything, the tremor stilled, as though he’d willed it into submission. “You mistake guilt for fear.”

“Guilt doesn’t shake,” Takeo said. “Guilt remembers.”

The acolyte let out a soft, involuntary gasp. Ōmura’s gaze snapped to him, sharp as a blade drawn from its sheath.

“You,” he said to the boy. “Kneel. Now.”

The acolyte dropped to his knees, forehead nearly touching the moss. His breath came fast, uneven.

Takeo stepped forward, close enough to smell the starch in Ōmura’s linen, the faint metallic tang of old blood on his breath. “He’s done nothing wrong.”

“He heard too much.”

“He heard the truth. That’s all.”

Ōmura’s right hand flexed at his side. “Truth has no place in the imperial garden.”

Takeo crouched beside the acolyte. “Then why do you still carve it?”

The boy flinched as Takeo’s fingers brushed his shoulder. A reassurance. A benediction.

Ōmura’s voice dropped to a whisper. “Walk away, monk. Before I decide you’re not worth the ink.”

Takeo rose slowly, his creased robes whispering against his ankles. “You could have walked away years ago. Before the plaque. Before the statue. Before the first lie.”

“There are no first lies,” Ōmura said. “Only the first truth that breaks us.”

Takeo turned his back on the architect, stepping toward the garden path. The acolyte scrambled up, keeping low, following at a distance.

“Wait.”

The single word stopped Takeo mid-step.

Ōmura’s hand hovered above the statue’s shoulder. Not touching. Almost.

“You never asked about the blade,” Ōmura said.

Takeo didn’t turn. “The blade is still.”

“Is it? Or is it waiting beneath the riverbed, wrapped in silk, its edge untouched by time?”

Takeo’s shoulders tightened. Just for a moment. “Some blades rust. Some cut air. Yours cut nothing.”

Ōmura’s laugh was bitter. “You always were a terrible liar, Takeo Hattori. Just not as terrible as me.”

Takeo finally turned. The morning light caught the tremor in Ōmura’s hand again, more pronounced now. A visible shiver that ran from shoulder to fingertips.

“You’re not the only one who remembers,” Takeo said.

Ōmura’s lips parted. Then closed. Then parted again. “What does that mean?”

“It means the past isn’t buried. It’s waiting.”

The acolyte made a small noise, a half-choked whimper. Both men ignored him.

Takeo walked to the edge of the rock garden, where the pines parted and the garden dropped into a dry stream bed. Beyond, the city hummed, unaware. The Grand Exhibition was in three days. The bronze Jizō would be unveiled. The court would clap. The marble would shine.

Ōmura followed, his sandals scuffing the moss. “You think you’re the only one with secrets in this garden?”

“I know I am.”

Ōmura stopped two paces behind him. “Then you don’t know Aya Fujimoto as well as you think.”

Takeo’s spine stiffened. The name was a blade between ribs.

“What does she want?” he asked, though he already feared the answer.

Ōmura’s voice was quiet. Almost gentle. “The same thing you do. For the past to rise. For the lie to collapse. But she wants it to bleed.”

Takeo turned slowly. The tremor in Ōmura’s hand had stopped. His expression was calm. Too calm.

“She’s not who you think,” Takeo said.

“No,” Ōmura agreed. “She’s worse.”

A gust of wind rattled the pine branches above them. The acolyte pressed closer to Takeo’s side, his eyes wide, terrified.

Then the first stone shifted.

Not the repaired Jizō. Not beneath their feet. But in the dry stream bed beyond the garden, where the earth had been disturbed—recently, deliberately—a single flat stone tilts, then drops into darkness. A hole opens where none was before. A draft rises from below, carrying the scent of wet earth and something older. Metallic. Sharp.

Ōmura’s eyes darted to the hole, then to Takeo.

Takeo stepped forward before he could stop himself, peering into the darkness. The acolyte whimpered again.

“That wasn’t here yesterday,” Ōmura said.

Takeo crouched, his fingers brushing the edge of the hole. The earth was cool. Too cool. As though something beneath had been breathing.

Then—

A sound.

Not from below.

From above.

The pine branch above them creaked. Splintered.

Ōmura’s head snapped up just as the branch broke.

The acolyte screamed.

The branch crashed down between them, its weight slamming into the moss where Takeo had stood a heartbeat before. Wood split. Needles rained.

Silence.

Then—laughter.

From the top of the garden wall, a figure leaned over, cloaked in black traveler’s robes, face hidden beneath a wide straw hat. A woman’s hand lifted in mock salute.

“Apologies, gentlemen,” the woman called, voice bright, amused. “I seem to have misjudged the angle.”

Takeo didn’t move. Didn’t blink.

He knew that voice.

Aya Fujimoto stepped back from the wall, the hem of her gray kosode fluttering in the wind. The straw hat tilted as she looked down at them.

“You dropped something,” she called. “Down the hole, I think.”

Ōmura’s gaze flicked between the hole and the woman on the wall. Then to Takeo.

Takeo stood slowly, his hands empty. His face unreadable.

“Come down,” he said.

Aya laughed again. “Oh no, monk. I don’t think I will.”

The wind stilled.

The acolyte’s breath hitched.

And the darkness below the shifted stone began to breathe back.
